This file contains the full text content of all pages on tampabankruptcymill.com.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).

## Tampa Bankruptcy Mill: Warning Signs and What the Court Data Shows
Source: https://tampabankruptcymill.com/

Skip to main content

tampabankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
How to Check
Warning Signs
Discharge Screener
Research

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project -- 75+ free domains, 2,000+ pages, $0 cost

Federal Court Data -- M.D. Fla.

Tampa Bankruptcy Mill:
Warning Signs and What the Court Data Shows

An independent, data-driven guide to identifying bankruptcy mill practices in the Tampa metropolitan area. Based on public federal court records from the Middle District of Florida.

What Is a Bankruptcy Mill?

A bankruptcy mill is a high-volume law firm that processes large numbers of bankruptcy cases with minimal individual attorney attention. The term is not a formal legal designation -- it describes a pattern of practice. Mill attorneys typically handle hundreds of active cases simultaneously, delegate most client contact to non-attorney staff, use template documents with minimal customization, and earn their fees regardless of whether the client's case succeeds or fails.

The Middle District of Florida is one of the geographically largest bankruptcy districts in the country, covering Tampa, Orlando, Jacksonville, and Fort Myers. The district's consumer bankruptcy docket is driven by Florida's combination of high housing costs, lack of state income tax, and a large population of retirees and service-industry workers.

Why this matters in Tampa: High filing volumes across the I-4 corridor with documented patterns of attorney concentration in Tampa, Orlando, and Jacksonville divisions.

The Tampa Bankruptcy Landscape

Bankruptcy cases in the Tampa area are filed in the Middle District of Florida (M.D. Fla.). This federal court handles all Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 consumer bankruptcy cases for the district. Understanding the local landscape is essential for anyone considering filing -- or wondering whether their current attorney is providing adequate representation.

Tampa's Chapter 13 market, like Miami's, features significant attorney concentration. The Middle District of Florida's multiple divisions create local sub-markets where a small number of firms dominate consumer filings. Dismissal rates across the district vary by division, but the Tampa division has drawn particular attention for high-volume practices.

Key Questions for Tampa Filers

1. What is your dismissal rate?

Any attorney should be able to tell you what percentage of their Chapter 13 cases are dismissed. If they don't know or won't say, that is itself a signal.

2. How many active cases do you personally handle?

An attorney handling 200+ active Chapter 13 cases may not have bandwidth for meaningful individual attention. Ask who will actually work on your case day-to-day.

3. Have you checked whether I'm eligible for a discharge?

Under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f), prior filers face waiting periods. This is a threshold question that should be answered before any filing.

4. Would Chapter 7 be a better fit for my situation?

Chapter 13 plans run 3-5 years with significant failure risk. If you qualify for Chapter 7, it may be the faster, safer option. Ask your attorney to explain why they recommend one chapter over the other.

5. How are your fees structured?

In Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically paid through the plan. Ask for the total fee upfront and compare it to the court-approved fee guidelines for the M.D. Fla..

How to Protect Yourself

Before hiring a bankruptcy attorney in Tampa, take these steps to evaluate the quality of representation you will receive:

Check PACER records. The Public Access to Court Electronic Records system allows you to search for any attorney's bankruptcy case history in the M.D. Fla.. Look for patterns: high dismissal rates, repeated case failures, or a volume of active cases that suggests inadequate individual attention.

Verify bar standing. The Florida Bar maintains public records of attorney licensing and disciplinary history. An attorney with a history of professional conduct complaints deserves additional scrutiny.

Check discharge eligibility. Before filing, use the free discharge eligibility screener to confirm that you are eligible for a discharge under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f). An attorney who files a case for a client who cannot receive a discharge is, at minimum, not performing adequate due diligence.

Consider free legal aid. Bay Area Legal Services provides free legal assistance to qualifying residents. If you cannot afford an attorney, a legal aid organization may be a better option than a mill.

Not all high-volume attorneys are mills. Volume alone does not make a mill. Some high-volume attorneys achieve excellent outcomes through efficient systems. The key metric is outcomes: what percentage of cases result in a successful discharge versus dismissal.

Explore More

Chapter 13 in Tampa

Red flags, plan failure patterns, and what to watch for in Tampa Chapter 13 cases.

Chapter 7 in Tampa

Costs, timeline, discharge data, and what to know before filing Chapter 7 in Tampa.

5 Warning Signs

Data-backed red flags that your bankruptcy attorney may be operating as a mill.

How to Check Your Attorney

Step-by-step guide to verifying your Tampa attorney's track record using public data.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is a bankruptcy mill?

A bankruptcy mill is a high-volume law firm that processes large numbers of bankruptcy cases with minimal individual attention. These firms often have dismissal rates significantly above the court average, delegate most work to non-attorney staff, and profit regardless of whether the client's case succeeds. The term has no formal legal definition, but the pattern is well-documented in federal court data and recognized by judges, trustees, and bar associations.

How do I know if my Tampa bankruptcy attorney is a mill?

Warning signs include: difficulty reaching your actual attorney, most communication through paralegals or assistants, missed filing deadlines, cookie-cutter plan terms without discussion of your specific situation, and a pattern of cases being dismissed. You can check dismissal rates and filing volumes through public court records at PACER. The Florida Bar can also tell you whether an attorney has any disciplinary history.

What court handles bankruptcy cases in Tampa?

Bankruptcy cases in the Tampa metropolitan area are filed in the Middle District of Florida (M.D. Fla.). This is a federal court -- bankruptcy is exclusively a federal matter under Title 11 of the United States Code. The court's public records are available through PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records).

How can I check a Tampa bankruptcy attorney's track record?

Public court records through PACER contain the outcome of every bankruptcy case. You can search by attorney name to see their full case history, including how many cases were dismissed versus discharged. The 1328f.com discharge screener provides district-level data. The Florida Bar can confirm licensing and disciplinary history.

What should I do if I think my Tampa attorney is a mill?

If you believe your attorney is not providing adequate representation: (1) document specific failures -- missed deadlines, unanswered communications, filing errors; (2) request a copy of your complete case file; (3) consult with another bankruptcy attorney for a second opinion; (4) file a complaint with the Florida Bar if warranted; (5) consider filing a motion to substitute counsel with the court. You have the right to competent representation.

Where can I get free legal help in Tampa?

Bay Area Legal Services provides free legal assistance to qualifying residents. The U.S. Trustee's office (Region 21) also maintains resources for debtors. You can also search for pro bono bankruptcy attorneys through the LawHelp.org directory.

About This Site

This site is an educational resource created by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization (determination pending). All information is derived from public federal court records, including the FJC Integrated Database and PACER.

This site does not identify individual attorneys by name. The purpose is to educate Tampa area residents about the warning signs of mill practices and provide tools to make informed decisions about bankruptcy representation.

This is not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Cited in Federal Rules Suggestion 26-BK-3

The research methodology behind this data has been submitted to and accepted by the Advisory Committee on Bankruptcy Rules of the Judicial Conference of the United States as Rules Suggestion 26-BK-3.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Mills in Tampa: Red Flags and What to Watch For
Source: https://tampabankruptcymill.com/chapter-13-tampa.html

Skip to main content

tampabankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
How to Check
Warning Signs
Discharge Screener
Research

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project -- 75+ free domains, 2,000+ pages, $0 cost

Chapter 13 -- M.D. Fla.

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Mills in Tampa:
Red Flags and What to Watch For

Chapter 13 is where bankruptcy mills do the most damage. A 3-5 year repayment plan means years of payments, and if the case is dismissed, you walk away with nothing. Here is what to watch for in Tampa.

Why Chapter 13 Is Where Mills Thrive

Chapter 13 bankruptcy requires a debtor to make monthly payments over 3 to 5 years. Attorney fees are typically paid through the plan, meaning the attorney gets paid even if the case is ultimately dismissed. This fee structure creates a perverse incentive: the attorney profits from filing the case, regardless of whether the client ever receives a discharge.

Tampa's Chapter 13 market, like Miami's, features significant attorney concentration. The Middle District of Florida's multiple divisions create local sub-markets where a small number of firms dominate consumer filings. Dismissal rates across the district vary by division, but the Tampa division has drawn particular attention for high-volume practices.

The "filed to fail" pattern: When an attorney files a Chapter 13 plan that is unlikely to succeed, the debtor still pays attorney fees, the attorney still earns their fee, and the client bears the full cost of failure. Identifying this pattern requires comparing individual attorney dismissal rates against the court average.

Chapter 13 Red Flags in Tampa

Based on patterns observed across federal bankruptcy courts, here are specific warning signs that your Tampa Chapter 13 attorney may be operating as a mill:

1. You never met with the attorney before filing

If your initial consultation was entirely with a paralegal or intake coordinator, and the attorney's first involvement was signing the petition, that is a red flag. The attorney has a duty to review your financial situation personally and ensure the filing is appropriate.

2. Your plan was filed with no discussion of terms

A Chapter 13 plan should reflect your specific financial situation -- your income, expenses, debts, and goals. If you were never asked about your monthly expenses, your vehicle needs, your income stability, or your long-term financial goals, the plan may be a template rather than a tailored document.

3. Monthly Operating Reports are late or missing

In some cases, Monthly Operating Reports (MORs) are required. Chronic failure to file MORs is a strong indicator of attorney disengagement. Ask your attorney whether your case requires MORs and whether they are current.

4. You cannot reach your attorney

If your calls go to voicemail, your emails go unanswered for days, and your only contact is with staff members who cannot answer substantive legal questions, your case may not be receiving adequate attention. You have a right to communicate with your attorney.

5. Your attorney also handles hundreds of other active cases

There is no specific case limit in Florida ethics rules, but an attorney handling 200+ active Chapter 13 cases while also maintaining new case intake may not have the bandwidth for meaningful individual attention. Ask how many active cases your attorney personally manages.

Document everything. If you observe these patterns, keep a record of every missed communication, every late filing, and every instance where your case was not handled as promised. This documentation is essential if you later file a complaint with the Florida Bar or seek a fee refund.

What to Do Before Filing Chapter 13 in Tampa

Ask about Chapter 7 first. Many filers who end up in Chapter 13 would have been better served by Chapter 7, which provides a faster discharge with no multi-year payment plan. Use the means test calculator to check your eligibility.

Check your discharge eligibility. Under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f), prior filers face waiting periods before they can receive a Chapter 13 discharge. An attorney who files a case for a client ineligible for discharge is not performing adequate due diligence.

Get a second opinion. If you have already been told that Chapter 13 is your only option, consider consulting with a different attorney -- or with Bay Area Legal Services -- to confirm that assessment.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 success rate in Tampa?

Chapter 13 success rates vary by district and by attorney. Nationally, roughly 33-40% of Chapter 13 cases result in a successful discharge. In the Middle District of Florida (M.D. Fla.), outcomes depend heavily on which attorney handles the case. Some high-volume practitioners have dismissal rates well above the court average, while others maintain rates below it.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Tampa?

Chapter 13 attorney fees in Tampa typically range from $3,000 to $5,500, paid through the bankruptcy plan over 3-5 years. The court filing fee is $313. Total out-of-pocket depends on your plan terms. Importantly, attorney fees are often paid even when the case is dismissed -- meaning the attorney earns their fee regardless of whether you receive a discharge.

Why are Chapter 13 cases dismissed in Tampa?

Common causes include failed plan payments, failure to file required documents, and failure to pay the filing fee. Plan failures can result from genuinely changed circumstances, but they can also result from poorly designed plans that were never realistic. When an attorney files a plan unlikely to succeed, the debtor bears the full cost of failure.

How long does Chapter 13 take in Tampa?

A Chapter 13 plan runs 3 to 5 years. Below-median-income debtors may qualify for a 3-year plan; above-median-income debtors must commit to 5 years. If the case is dismissed before completion, the debtor receives no discharge and may have spent years making payments with no debt relief.

Can I switch from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in Tampa?

Yes, conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 is generally available under 11 U.S.C. § 1307(a) as a matter of right, provided the case was not previously converted from Chapter 7. This can be important if your circumstances change. However, Chapter 7 has its own eligibility requirements including the means test.

About This Data

This site does not identify individual attorneys by name. All information is derived from public federal court records, including the FJC Integrated Database and PACER. This is an educational resource, not legal advice.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Tampa: Costs, Timeline and What to Know
Source: https://tampabankruptcymill.com/chapter-7-tampa.html

Skip to main content

tampabankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
How to Check
Warning Signs
Discharge Screener
Research

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project -- 75+ free domains, 2,000+ pages, $0 cost

Chapter 7 -- M.D. Fla.

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Tampa:
Costs, Timeline, and What to Know

Chapter 7 is the fastest form of consumer bankruptcy, with 90%+ discharge rates and a typical timeline of 3-4 months. Here is what Tampa filers need to know.

Chapter 7 Basics for Tampa Filers

Chapter 7 bankruptcy provides a relatively quick discharge of most unsecured debts -- credit cards, medical bills, personal loans -- typically within 3 to 4 months of filing. Unlike Chapter 13, there is no multi-year repayment plan. The trade-off is that non-exempt assets may be liquidated, though most consumer filers retain all of their property under applicable exemptions.

Florida's unlimited homestead exemption makes Chapter 7 an attractive option for homeowners. Chapter 7 discharge rates in the Middle District of Florida are consistently strong. The state's lack of income tax means that the means test relies more heavily on actual expense calculations.

Key advantage over Chapter 13: Chapter 7 discharge rates typically exceed 90%. Chapter 13 completion rates are often below 50%. For qualifying filers, Chapter 7 is the faster, cheaper, and far less risky option.

What Chapter 7 Costs in Tampa

The costs of filing Chapter 7 in the Middle District of Florida (M.D. Fla.) include:

Court filing fee: $338

Set by the Judicial Conference. Can be paid in installments or waived for qualifying low-income filers.

Attorney fees: $1,000 - $2,500

Varies by attorney and case complexity. Paid upfront before filing. Get quotes from multiple attorneys and compare.

Credit counseling: $30 - $100

Two required courses (pre-filing and pre-discharge). Many providers offer online courses at the lower end of this range.

Total cost for a typical Tampa Chapter 7 case: $1,400 to $2,900. This is significantly less than the total cost of a Chapter 13 case, which includes higher attorney fees plus years of plan payments.

The Tampa Chapter 7 Timeline

A typical Chapter 7 case in Tampa follows this timeline:

Day 0: Filing

Petition filed with the M.D. Fla.. Automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping most collection activity.

Days 21-50: 341 Meeting

Meeting of creditors conducted by the Chapter 7 trustee. Typically lasts 5-10 minutes for straightforward consumer cases.

Day 60 after 341: Discharge

If no objections are filed, the discharge order is entered approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting.

Total: 3-4 months

From filing to discharge, most straightforward Chapter 7 cases close within 3 to 4 months.

Do You Qualify? The Means Test

Chapter 7 eligibility is determined by the means test under 11 U.S.C. § 707(b). The test compares your household income to the Florida state median for your family size.

Below median income: You generally qualify for Chapter 7 automatically.

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines whether you can file Chapter 7 or must file Chapter 13 instead.

Beware of steering. Some attorneys recommend Chapter 13 even when the client qualifies for Chapter 7, because Chapter 13 attorney fees are higher and paid through the plan. Always ask whether you qualify for Chapter 7 and, if so, why Chapter 13 is being recommended instead.

Mill Warning Signs in Chapter 7

While bankruptcy mills primarily profit from Chapter 13, Chapter 7 is not immune from quality issues. Watch for these signs in Tampa:

Steering toward Chapter 13. If you qualify for Chapter 7 but your attorney recommends Chapter 13 without a clear, case-specific explanation, that is a red flag.

Template petitions. Your bankruptcy petition should reflect your actual financial situation in detail. If schedules appear to use round numbers or generic expense categories, the petition may not be adequately individualized.

No discussion of exemptions. Florida exemption planning is critical to protecting your assets in Chapter 7. An attorney who does not discuss exemptions in detail may not be providing adequate representation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 bankruptcy cost in Tampa?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338. Attorney fees in Tampa typically range from $1,000 to $2,500, paid upfront before filing. Total cost including credit counseling ($15-50 for each of two required courses) is typically $1,400 to $2,900.

What is the Chapter 7 discharge rate in Tampa?

Chapter 7 discharge rates are generally above 90% across most federal bankruptcy courts, including the M.D. Fla.. Less than 1% of Chapter 7 cases are typically dismissed. This makes Chapter 7 significantly less risky than Chapter 13 for qualifying filers.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Tampa?

A typical Chapter 7 case takes 3 to 4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors is held 21-50 days after filing, and the discharge is typically entered about 60 days after the 341 meeting, assuming no objections.

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Tampa?

Eligibility depends on the means test under 11 U.S.C. § 707(b). If your household income is below the Florida state median for your family size, you generally qualify. If above median, a detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility. Check your eligibility at meanstest.org.

Can I file Chapter 7 if I already filed bankruptcy before?

You can file Chapter 7 at any time, but you cannot receive a discharge if you received a Chapter 7 discharge within the last 8 years or a Chapter 13 discharge within the last 6 years (11 U.S.C. § 727(a)(8)-(9)). Check your eligibility at 1328f.com.

About This Data

## How to Check Your Tampa Bankruptcy Attorney's Track Record
Source: https://tampabankruptcymill.com/how-to-check.html

Skip to main content

tampabankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
How to Check
Warning Signs
Discharge Screener
Research

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project -- 75+ free domains, 2,000+ pages, $0 cost

Attorney Verification -- M.D. Fla.

How to Check Your Tampa Bankruptcy
Attorney's Track Record

Every bankruptcy case is a public record. You have the right -- and the tools -- to verify your attorney's track record before you sign a retainer agreement.

Step 1: Check PACER

PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) is the federal court system's electronic records platform. Every bankruptcy case filed in the Middle District of Florida (M.D. Fla.) is available through PACER.

What to look for:

Search for your attorney by name. Review their case history, focusing on:

Case volume: How many cases do they file per year? Per month?

Dismissal rate: What percentage of their Chapter 13 cases are dismissed?

Case types: Do they file mostly Chapter 13, Chapter 7, or a mix?

Pattern of failures: Do cases fail at the same stages (plan confirmation, payment default)?

PACER costs $0.10 per page (with a $3.00 cap per document). Quarterly fees under $30 are waived. A basic attorney search is inexpensive and provides essential due diligence.

Step 2: Check Bar Standing

Verify that your attorney is licensed and in good standing with the Florida Bar. This free search will show:

Current license status (active, suspended, disbarred)

Any public disciplinary actions

Years of practice and bar admission date

An attorney with a history of professional conduct complaints -- even if not formally disciplined -- may warrant additional scrutiny.

Step 3: Check Discharge Eligibility

Before hiring any attorney, verify your own eligibility for a discharge. Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check whether prior filings create a bar under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f) or § 727(a)(8)-(9).

If you are not eligible for a discharge, an ethical attorney should tell you this before accepting your case. An attorney who files a case for a client ineligible for discharge is, at minimum, not performing adequate due diligence.

Step 4: Get a Second Opinion

If anything about your attorney's track record concerns you, consult with a second attorney. Many bankruptcy attorneys offer free initial consultations. You can also contact Bay Area Legal Services for free legal guidance.

Trust your instincts. If your attorney is hard to reach before the case is even filed, communication is unlikely to improve after filing. If they cannot answer basic questions about their dismissal rate or case volume, they may not be tracking their own outcomes.

Step 5: Know Your Rights

As a bankruptcy debtor, you have the right to:

Communicate directly with your attorney (not just staff)

Receive a copy of your complete case file at any time

Be informed of all deadlines and court dates

Substitute counsel by filing a motion with the court

File a complaint with the Florida Bar

Request a fee review by the court under 11 U.S.C. § 329

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I look up a Tampa bankruptcy attorney on PACER?

Go to pacer.gov, select the M.D. Fla., and use the attorney search function. You can search by attorney name to see all cases they have filed. Review the docket entries and case dispositions to understand their track record.

What dismissal rate should I be concerned about?

Context matters, but generally a Chapter 13 dismissal rate significantly above the court average for the M.D. Fla. deserves scrutiny. An attorney whose dismissal rate is 10+ percentage points above the court average may not be providing adequate case management.

How do I file a complaint against a Tampa bankruptcy attorney?

Contact the Florida Bar. You can file a complaint regarding any attorney licensed in Florida. Keep records of all communications, missed deadlines, and other evidence of inadequate representation.

Can I check my attorney's disciplinary history?

Yes. The Florida Bar maintains public records of attorney licensing status and any disciplinary actions. This search is free and available online.

Is there a way to check for free without PACER?

The 1328f.com discharge screener provides district-level data on outcomes. For individual attorney records, PACER is the primary source. Some documents are also available through the RECAP Archive, a free repository of PACER documents.

About This Site

This site does not identify individual attorneys by name. It provides tools and instructions for Tampa area residents to conduct their own due diligence using public records. This is an educational resource, not legal advice.

Stay updated on new datasets and research findings

## 5 Warning Signs of a Bankruptcy Mill in Tampa
Source: https://tampabankruptcymill.com/warning-signs.html

Skip to main content

tampabankruptcymill.com
☰

Home
Chapter 13 Data
Chapter 7 Data
How to Check
Warning Signs
Discharge Screener
Research

Consumer Protection -- Tampa

5 Warning Signs of a Bankruptcy Mill
in Tampa

Based on patterns documented across federal bankruptcy courts nationwide. If you see these signs at a Tampa bankruptcy firm, proceed with caution.

Warning Sign #1: You Cannot Reach Your Attorney

The most common complaint against high-volume bankruptcy firms is inaccessibility. If your calls consistently go to voicemail, your emails take days to receive a response, and your only point of contact is a paralegal or intake coordinator, your case may not be receiving adequate attorney attention.

The Florida Rules of Professional Conduct require attorneys to maintain reasonable communication with clients. An attorney who is too busy to return calls may be handling more cases than they can competently manage.

What to do: Document every unreturned call and unanswered email with dates and times. If the pattern persists, raise it directly with the attorney (in writing) and note their response. This documentation is essential for any future complaint to the Florida Bar.

Warning Sign #2: Cookie-Cutter Plan Terms

A Chapter 13 plan should be tailored to your specific financial situation. If your attorney never asked about your monthly expenses in detail, never discussed your vehicle needs, your medical costs, your childcare expenses, or your income stability, the plan may be a template rather than a document designed for your case.

Cookie-cutter plans are a hallmark of mill practices. They are fast to produce and require minimal attorney time, but they often fail because they do not reflect the debtor's actual ability to make payments over 3-5 years.

Warning Sign #3: Steering Toward Chapter 13

Chapter 13 attorney fees are typically higher than Chapter 7 fees, and they are paid through the plan over time rather than upfront. This creates a financial incentive for attorneys to recommend Chapter 13 even when Chapter 7 would be more appropriate.

If you qualify for Chapter 7 but your attorney insists on Chapter 13 without a clear, case-specific reason, get a second opinion. Valid reasons for Chapter 13 over Chapter 7 include protecting non-exempt assets, curing mortgage arrears, or paying priority debts. "Chapter 13 is better for you" without specifics is not adequate counsel.

The numbers speak. Chapter 7 discharge rates exceed 90%. Chapter 13 completion rates are often below 50%. For qualifying filers, the risk profile of Chapter 13 is dramatically higher. Make sure you understand why Chapter 13 is being recommended for your specific situation.

Warning Sign #4: No Discharge Eligibility Check

Under 11 U.S.C. § 1328(f), prior bankruptcy filers face waiting periods before they can receive a discharge. An attorney who files a case without checking whether the client is eligible for a discharge is not performing basic due diligence.

This is a threshold question. If you are not eligible for a discharge, filing bankruptcy costs you time, money, and a mark on your credit report -- with zero benefit. Ask your attorney directly: "Have you checked whether I am eligible for a discharge under Section 1328(f)?"

Warning Sign #5: The Attorney Won't Share Their Dismissal Rate

Every attorney's bankruptcy case outcomes are public record. If your attorney cannot or will not tell you their Chapter 13 dismissal rate, that is a significant red flag. An attorney who is proud of their outcomes will share them. An attorney who avoids the question may have something to hide.

You can verify their self-reported numbers through PACER. See our step-by-step guide for how to look up any Tampa attorney's track record.

What to Do If You See These Signs

If you recognize multiple warning signs at your current Tampa bankruptcy firm:

1. Document everything

Keep records of missed communications, late filings, and template documents. Dates and specifics matter.

2. Request your case file

You have the right to a copy of your complete case file at any time. Make the request in writing.

3. Get a second opinion

Consult with another attorney or contact Bay Area Legal Services for guidance.

4. File a motion to substitute counsel

If you want to change attorneys mid-case, file a motion with the M.D. Fla.. Courts generally grant substitution for good cause.

5. File a bar complaint if warranted

The Florida Bar investigates complaints of attorney misconduct. Your complaint helps protect future clients.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the most common warning sign of a Tampa bankruptcy mill?

The single most reliable indicator is the combination of high case volume and high dismissal rates. An attorney who files hundreds of cases per year and has a dismissal rate well above the court average is exhibiting the classic mill pattern: volume over quality.

Can a bankruptcy mill attorney be disciplined?

Yes. If an attorney provides inadequate representation, you can file a complaint with the Florida Bar. Common grounds include failure to communicate, failure to pursue the client's objectives, and charging unreasonable fees. The bankruptcy court can also review fees under 11 U.S.C. § 329.

Is it too late to switch attorneys after filing?

No. You can file a motion to substitute counsel at any point during your bankruptcy case. The court will generally grant substitution if there is good cause. You may also be entitled to a refund of fees already paid if the original attorney provided inadequate service.

Are all high-volume attorneys mills?

No. Some high-volume attorneys achieve excellent outcomes through efficient systems and experienced staff. The key distinction is outcomes. A high-volume attorney with a low dismissal rate is providing value through efficiency. A high-volume attorney with a high dismissal rate is processing cases without adequate attention to individual client needs.

How do I find a good bankruptcy attorney in Tampa?

Start with the National Association of Consumer Bankruptcy Attorneys directory, which lists attorneys who specialize in consumer bankruptcy. Check each candidate's track record on PACER. Contact Bay Area Legal Services for referrals. Ask any prospective attorney about their dismissal rate and case volume before signing a retainer.

About This Site

This site does not identify individual attorneys by name. It provides educational information for Tampa area residents about identifying mill practices using public data. This is not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

Stay updated on new datasets and research findings

No spam. No marketing. Just data.

This site is free and open-source. Donations support the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit (determination pending), funding PACER access fees and bankruptcy court transparency research.
